data.title: Japan futures fall to three-week low on US tariff hit
data.description:
• Japanese rubber futures fell to their lowest since March 11 on Thursday after U.S. President Donald Trump announced a sweeping set of tariffs, dampening the global demand outlook and escalating a trade war.

  • The Osaka Exchange (OSE) September rubber contract , slid 3.92% to finish at 331.3 yen ($2.26) per kg, closing near a three-week low of 331.1 yen.

  • The May rubber contract on the Shanghai Futures Exchange (SHFE) slumped 3.18% to 16,295 yuan ($2,232.99) per metric ton.

  • Prices tumbled to a near 7-month low at 16,310 yuan earlier in the session, their lowest since September 6, 2024.

  • The most active May butadiene rubber contract on the SHFE (SHBRv1) sank 4.04% to 13,170 yuan ($1,804.75) per ton.

  • "Tariff announcements saw rubber futures down across the board, showing that the market is factoring impact to demand," said Farah Miller, founder of independent rubber-focused data firm Helixtap Technologies.

  • Trump on Wednesday unveiled a 10% tariff on most goods imported to the U.S., worsening a trade war that threatens to drive up U.S. inflation and stall global economic growth.

  • Meanwhile, Trump's 25% auto tariffs, kicking in April 3, will cover hundreds of billions of dollars worth of imports of vehicles and auto parts imports annually.

  • Automobile sales could influence the intensity of automobile manufacturing, which involves using rubber-made tyres.

  • In response to fresh tariffs, equities in Beijing and Tokyo fell to multi-month lows, with China vowing countermeasures on Thursday.

  • In the coming week, prices will remain volatile as market participants look for new support and resistance levels, added Miller.

  • The front-month rubber contract on Singapore Exchange's SICOM platform for May delivery last traded at 185.2 U.S. cents per kg, down 4.1%.

  • China's financial markets will be closed on Friday for a public holiday. Trading will resume on Monday, April 7.

($1 = 146.8500 yen)

($1 = 7.2974 Chinese yuan)

data.title: Optimal Fed response to tariffs? Ease policy: McGeever
data.description:
Focus on the "stag". Ride out the "flation". That may be the Federal Reserve's optimal plan for handling the new wave of tariffs coming from the Trump administration.

With details of U.S. President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs now emerging, all eyes will soon turn to the Fed's and other central banks' response to the president's "liberation day" duties that could leave them in quite a bind.

It's generally agreed that tariffs are damaging to growth and inflationary, initially at least. So how should central bankers react? Do they cut interest rates to prop up a stagnating economy or raise them to cool fiery price pressures?

According to a Minneapolis Fed working paper published last month, the answer is clearly the former. The authors find that the "optimal" policy response to tariffs is not just to look through the inflationary impact and keep rates steady, but to go even further and ease policy.

"The optimal monetary response is to stimulate the economy, raising aggregate income and boosting demand for imported goods," wrote Minneapolis Fed economist Javier Bianchi and University of Wisconsin-Madison assistant professor Louphou Coulibaly.

The optimal response, they argue, "is expansionary, letting inflation rise above and beyond the direct effects of tariffs. This result holds regardless of whether tariffs apply to consumption goods or intermediate inputs, whether the shock is temporary or permanent."

ADVERSE EFFECTS

This all runs counter to the commonly held belief that pouring fuel on an inflationary fire is essentially the most dangerous thing a central banker can do, as it risks "unanchoring" inflation expectations.

But the authors argue that history simply doesn't show this to be the case. Instead, the data suggests that to mitigate the slump in imports as tariffs bite, the central bank must stimulate economic activity, lift employment and boost income. Policymakers must be prepared to "tolerate some overheating," the authors contend.

This conclusion reflects another important lesson from economic history: tariffs are pretty bad for growth.

A 2020 study using aggregated data for 151 countries from 1963 through 2014 found that tariffs have "economically- and statistically-significant adverse effects" on growth.

The impact is "persistent" and increases over time, the researchers found. Their baseline model suggests that a 3.6 percentage points increase in tariffs results in a 0.4% decline in economic output five years later.

They actually found that the projected longer-term effect of tariffs on GDP was higher than the estimated medium-term impacts, but they limited their study to a five-year time horizon "in an effort to be conservative."

PROCEED WITH CAUTION

Right now, Fed officials are also erring on the side of caution. In their revised economic projections released last month, they maintained their forecast for two quarter-point rate cuts this year, although a hawkish underlying shift in the "dot plot" of officials' individual projections moved the median closer to one cut.

And Fed Chair Jerome Powell has been at pains to be neutral and non-committal on the question of tariffs, insisting that, before acting, he and his colleagues must wait and see what the actual impact is on activity, prices and employment.

Fed Governor Chris Waller, however, was a bit bolder in a speech to the OECD in Paris in January, stating: "If, as I expect, tariffs do not have a significant or persistent effect on inflation, they are unlikely to affect my view of appropriate monetary policy."

The market is certainly focusing on the "stag" more than the "flation". U.S. businesses are reporting the highest factory gate prices in years and consumer inflation expectations are the highest in decades, according to some measures, yet bond yields are falling and interest rate futures are pricing in multiple cuts this year and into 2026.

Investors appear to be betting that, if the tariff push comes to the recession shove, the Fed will focus on stimulating growth. History suggests they're right.

(The opinions expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.)

data.title: News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Thursday at 3 AM ET
data.description:
Asian Markets Roiled by 'Liberation Day' Tariffs

Asian equities tumbled as markets awoke to see some of their worst fears come true: a slate of sweeping U.S. tariffs that could inflict economic damage and disrupt industries.

Trump Tariffs Aim to Bring Down Curtain on Era of Globalization

The president wants companies to return production to the U.S., but it won't be easy.

EU's Von Der Leyen Vows Response to Trump's 20% Tariffs

European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said it is not too late to engage in talks with the U.S.

China Services Gauge Shows Acceleration in Activity Expansion

China's services-sector activity expanded at a faster pace in March, thanks to improving demand boosted by government stimulus measures, according to a private survey.

Trump Officials Gave Conflicting Tariff Rates for China. White House Calls the Issue a 'Pointless Aside.'

Confusion reigned even among some White House officials about what rate the approximately $440 billion in Chinese imports would face.

Some GOP Senators Break With Trump to Reject Canada Tariffs

The Senate passed a resolution to undo the emergency declaration the president used to impose the tariffs, but the measure has been blocked in the Republican-controlled House.

What to Know About Trump's Latest Tariffs

President Trump unveiled a new tariff regime, including 10% across-the-board tariffs on all imports and even higher rates for some nations. Here's the latest.

The Stock Market Pain Is Just Getting Started. 'This is How You Sabotage the World's Economic Engine.'

"Liberation Day" wasn't the clearing event investors hopped for. That isn't good news for stocks-in the short run.

Trump's tariffs are 'a game changer,' not only for U.S. economy but for the world

President Donald Trump's new tariff plan is a "tsunami" that will slam into the global economy and have repercussions that are hard to quantify, economists said Wednesday.

The Panama Canal Is More Important Than Ever. The U.S. Can't Do Without It.

Trump wants control over the canal, which is key for transporting U.S. petroleum products to growing Asia markets.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Thursday at 3 AM ET
data.description:
AppLovin and Amazon Emerge as TikTok Bidders Ahead of Trump's Deadline

President Trump was briefed Wednesday on a framework to keep the video-sharing app operational.

Zuckerberg Lobbies Trump to Avoid Meta Antitrust Trial

Meta's chief executive was at the White House on Wednesday, his third visit during Trump's presidency, according to people familiar with the matter.

Apple's stock is getting pounded on tariff news. Don't freak out just yet.

Apple has a loyal customer base, strong gross margins - and a history of getting tariff exemptions.

RH's stock slammed by new tariffs and outlook, but CEO offers this praise for Trump administration

Shares of RH fell sharply after hours on Wednesday after the luxury furniture chain's full-year sales outlook came in shy of expectations, amid a forecast of what it characterized as a "higher-risk business environment this year" due to tariffs, seesawing markets and inflation.

Venezuela Bondholders Find Path to Repayment in Citgo Auction

The leading bid for Citgo favors creditors whose collateral rights over the refiner are in dispute.

Macy's $150 Million Accounting Error Wrongly Boosted Executives' Pay

The company erroneously overpaid C-suite members by about $600,000 in 2023 as a result of an accounting error that hid $150 million in delivery costs.

Tesla's Global Vehicle Deliveries Sank 13% in First Quarter

The decline is further evidence that a growing consumer backlash against the brand and CEO Elon Musk is hurting the automaker's business.

Siemens Agrees to Buy Dotmatics for $5.1 Billion, Expanding AI Portfolio to Life Sciences

The deal extends Siemens's artificial-intelligence-powered product-lifecycle-management portfolio and increases the company's total addressable market in industrial software by $11 billion.

Hims & Hers Zepbound Offering Won't Bring Needed Sales Boom, Analysts Say

Investors worry patients won't make that price leap and that Hims, which has built its business on affordable healthcare, will struggle to meet its revenue goal.

Trump Media Shares Sink After Clearing Path for President's Trust to Sell Stake

The stock was recently headed for its lowest close of the year.

data.title: Market Talk Roundup: Latest on U.S. Politics
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering U.S. politics. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0315 ET - Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni says that officials will do whatever they can to avoid a trade war with the U.S. after President Trump imposed a 20% levy on EU imports on Wednesday. The tariffs are wrong and not in the interest of either party, Meloni says. "We will do everything we can to work on an agreement with the U.S., with the aim of averting a trade war that would inevitably weaken the West in favor of other global players," the premier says. She adds her government will act in Italy's interests and also work with European partners. (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

0313 ET - The fear around U.S. tariffs is relative for European insurers, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods says in a research note. "We view them as second-order relative to the wider challenges posed to economies and capital markets, especially for global insurance businesses that can readjust as trade flows and economic momentum evolve," William Hawkins writes. The first order of worry may be the risk to non-life insurers from higher prices in the U.S. and the inflationary consequences. In the long term, a world of higher inflation and yields is good for insurance fundamentals, he notes. Companies in KBW's coverage generally have strong balance sheets to take advantage of opportunities and keep returning attractive payouts to shareholders, he notes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0301 ET - Luxury companies, watchmakers and midsize industrial companies seem the most exposed among Swiss exporters to direct impacts from the Trump administration's plan to set a 31% tariff on imports from Switzerland, Vontobel analysts say. Swiss pharmaceutical exports are partially exempt and so are some semiconductors, Vontobel says. Meanwhile, large companies with U.S. production could even benefit, according to Vontobel. "Pure domestic players are a relative safer haven, the analysts say. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0256 ET - Irish bank shares are set to remain volatile as the potential implications of U.S. tariffs on European growth and interest rates play out, Citi says in a research note. The U.S.'s 20% tariffs on goods imports from the EU excludes pharmaceuticals, which might provide a short-term relief for Ireland given that two-thirds of its 2023 U.S. exports were linked to the sector, analyst Borja Ramirez Segura writes. "For the time being, it remains unclear whether the baseline 10% tariff will be applied to drugs, and there may be targeted tariffs on the pharma sector in the near future," he says. AIB Group is best placed among domestic peers in case of weaker economic growth given its higher capital and overlay provisions, the analyst adds. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0244 ET - President Trump's retaliatory tariffs were much higher than expected, and this has investors fearing a significant weakening of global growth, Jyske Markets says. "Investors are fleeing stocks, and especially cyclical sectors, and are seeking protection in bonds and other safe havens such as the yen." Investors will today be watching for reactions from the affected countries in the form of retaliation as well as expansionary fiscal policy or signals of interest rate cuts, it says. Jyske Markets says a quick calculation suggests the tariffs could send the U.S. into recession if they are maintained for a longer period. At the same time, core inflation will rise significantly, and this combination will put the Fed under serious pressure, it says. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0241 ET - The U.S. tariff rate of 31% on Swiss exports is excessively high and represents a serious burden to business, Swiss business lobby Economiesuisse says. "The new tariffs make Swiss exports more expensive, weaken the competitiveness of companies and impact the investment climate," the association says. "This is happening at a time when export prospects in other sales markets are already gloomy." The association argues that Switzerland's import tariffs are already lower than those of the U.S. At around 53 billion Swiss francs ($60 billion), the U.S. is Switzerland's most important export market, according to Economiesuisse. (sarah.sloat@wsj.com)

0238 ET - European stocks are expected to open sharply lower after President Trump announced sweeping tariffs that were greater than market commentators had expected. IG calls the Stoxx Europe 600 index down 1.9%; Germany's DAX index down 1.7%; France's CAC 40 down 1.5%; and the U.K.'s FTSE 100 down 1.2%. The U.S. will impose 10% across-the-board tariffs on all imports and even higher rates for some nations, including 20% for Europe. "Markets have seen a strong risk-off reaction to the tariff announcement," Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid says in a note. "Trump's comments did leave the door open for potential negotiations to lower tariffs but his executive order also left room for further escalation," he says. (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0217 ET - Trade association UK Steel says it remains deeply concerned by the imposition of tariffs by U.S. President Trump. "The U.K. government must continue its efforts to strike a deal with the U.S., but we recognize that this requires willingness from both sides," the trade body says. The industry can't wait any longer for government support to address a deepening crisis in the sector, the body says. Steel from the U.K. to the U.S. will continue to face a 25% tariff as introduced in March, it says. Trump announced a slew of additional tariffs Wednesday, adding a 10% import tax on all other goods imported to the U.S. from the country. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0155 ET - Trade tariffs have shortened the odds on a fresh cut to eurozone interest rates when the European Central Bank's rate setters meet later this month, ING says. The EU faces duties of at least 20% on all its exports to the U.S., part of a major global tariff package unveiled Wednesday by President Trump. While some ECB policymakers have lately hinted that a pause in rate-cuts might be due as inflationary pressures remain, ING points to the serious trade and confidence shock the tariffs represent, with little prospect of quick and effective negotiations to stop them. "The chances that the ECB will want to push the policy interest rate further into neutral territory in two weeks have clearly increased," the bank's economists tell clients in a note. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0148 ET - It is uncertain if U.S. tariffs might increase and whether President Trump is willing to negotiate them down, investment strategist at Quilter Lindsay James writes. Historically, tariffs have rarely delivered positive outcomes and the there are signs that the U.S economy may be starting to slow, James adds. Given the high levels of uncertainty, investors should remain calm and stay invested for the long term, James writes.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0144 ET - A 20% tariff on all exports to the U.S. is a major blow to the European economy, economists at Dutch bank ING warn in a note, trimming their forecasts for eurozone growth ahead. President Trump on Wednesday set out duties on European imports as part of a major package of tariffs on global goods aiming, he says, to protect domestic manufacture. In Europe, the consequences will hinge much on counter-measures but will certainly in the near term be depressive for growth, ING says. There are bright spots ahead for the continent's economies from planned stimulus, but that will take time, the bank says. As such, the 20 members of the eurozone are likely to book GDP growth of just 0.6% this year and 1.0% in 2026, down from projections of 0.7% and 1.4%, respectively, ING says. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0143 ET - The U.K. Government needs to double efforts and work urgently to negotiate a mutually beneficial economic agreement with the U.S. to reverse the tariffs charged on exported goods, chief executive of manufacturing trade body Make UK, Stephen Phipson, says. The tariffs will destroy decades of integrated supply chains connecting the U.K. with U.S. through other trading partners who are also impacted by the tariffs, he says. "This isn't the time for a trade war, we need to find a way to mitigate against these crippling tariffs at speed," Phipson says. (ian.walker@wsj.com.)

data.description: Italian prime minister Giorgia Meloni says that officials will do whatever they can to avoid a trade war with the U.S. after President Trump imposed a 20% levy on EU imports on Wednesday. The tariffs are wrong and not in the interest of either party, Meloni says. "We will do everything we can to work on an agreement with the U.S., with the aim of averting a trade war that would inevitably weaken the West in favor of other global players," the premier says. data.title: European Stocks Tumble
data.description:
Major European bourses tumbled on Thursday, with both the STOXX 50 and STOXX 600 sinking over 2%, amid a global stock sell-off following President Trump’s latest tariff announcement.

The move triggered a broad flight to safety and deepened concerns over its impact on the global economy.

The European Union will face tariffs of up to 20%, prompting European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen to warn that the measures would deal “a major blow” to the global economy.

She also confirmed that the EU was preparing countermeasures.

Losses were broad-based, with basic resources and banks among the worst-performing sectors.

On the corporate front, shares of Siemens, Airbus, Essilor, Schneider Electric, BNP Paribas, UniCredit, and Intesa Sanpaolo all dropped more than 3%.

ASML Holding slid nearly 2.9%, while LVMH declined more than 2%.

data.title: Spanish service activity growth slows down in March, PMI shows
data.description:
Spain's service sector's activity kept expanding in March albeit at its slowest pace since November, a survey showed on Thursday.

The HCOB Spain Services PMI Business Activity Index fell to 54.7 in March from 56.2 in February, according to S&P Global. The index was still above the 50 threshold which signals growth, though it was at its lowest since a 53.1 reading in November.

Spain's services sector has been expanding continuously since September 2023, on the back of strong demand both in the country and abroad.

"Business activity and incoming business growth remain comfortably high, although index values softened compared to the previous month," said Hamburg Commercial Bank economist Jonas Feldhusen.

"Nevertheless, overall service demand is constantly growing, with new export business even accelerating on the back of increased tourism inflows as panellists reported," he added.

Employment in the sector rose at a strong pace, as firms remained optimistic about future activity.

Operating costs for service providers increased sharply, driven by higher staffing expenses, although input price inflation eased from February's one-year high.

Despite the moderation in growth, firms are confident about the year ahead, with business expectations slightly above the long-term average.

The service sector's resilience contrasts with ongoing weakness in Spain's manufacturing sector, which contracted in March for the consecutive month, a sister survey showed earlier this week.

The Spanish government expects the economy as a whole to expand 2.6% this year, much faster than most of the rest of the euro area.

data.title: REP. AYANNA PRESSLEY (D-MA) IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC PRIME
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 02, 2025

NEWS EVENT

REP. AYANNA PRESSLEY (D-MA)

REP. AYANNA PRESSLEY (D-MA) IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC PRIME

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

REP. AYANNA PRESSLEY (D-MA) IS INTERVIEWED ON MSNBC PRIME

APRIL 2, 2025

SPEAKERS:

REP. AYANNA PRESSLEY (D-MA)

ALICIA MENENDEZ, MSNBC HOST

ALICIA MENENDEZ, MSNBC HOST: Ever since the U.S. Supreme Court overturned Roe v. Wade in 2022, Republicans have sought to further erode access to reproductive health care.

And, today, the Supreme Court heard a new case on whether South Carolina Governor Henry McMaster can strip Planned Parenthood of its Medicaid funding in the state. McMaster targeted Planned Parenthood despite the fact that their abortion services are not covered by Medicaid.

Additionally, South Carolina bans abortion after six weeks, when most women don't yet know they are pregnant. Planned Parenthood is a popular health care provider for low-income patients because they offer a number of services, including basic physical exams, cancer screenings, and other screenings at flexible hours and with same-day appointments.

If the court rules in favor of South Carolina, other states will most likely follow, dramatically slashing access to health care for patients in rural states. The conservative justices appeared divided over the question before the court.

Joining me now, Massachusetts Congresswoman Ayanna Pressley, who was outside the Supreme Court earlier today for today's oral arguments. She's the co-chair of the House Reproductive Freedom Caucus.

Congresswoman, it is good to see you.

This case, very technical, what does it actually mean for your constituents?

REP. AYANNA PRESSLEY (D-MA): First, let me just do a little right-sizing here and just make sure people understand that, despite the fact that Donald Trump has declared this liberation day, this — that is an incredible irony, when Donald Trump and his co-conspirators, including the governor of South Carolina, are participating actively in an anti-freedom agenda.

They want to deny free speech. They want to control what books you read. They want to whitewash and erase history. And they want to control your bodily autonomy. And they want to control where nearly eight million Medicaid recipients receive their health care.

Health care is a fundamental human right. Moreover, half-a-century ago, Congress passed a law giving Medicaid patients the right to access health care, including routine primary care, from any qualified provider, which Planned Parenthood clinicians clearly fit in that criteria.

But they seek to deny them this basic and essential health care, understanding that the most marginalized, rural, underserved communities, low-income people, black, brown, indigenous, LGBTQ, would stand to be the most impacted by this denial of care.

The law is on our side. The law is on the side of the people. And that's why I went there today, because we're not taking anything for granted. Hundreds from around the country came to make their voices heard in this fight for that which is an essential fundamental freedom, health care.

MENENDEZ: Congresswoman, given that this is before the Supreme Court, how much confidence do you have in the court right now?

PRESSLEY: Well, again, the rule of law here is on our side. The Supreme Court simply needs to uphold the law, and they need to stand with the people.

Your right to access health care should not be denied simply because of how you pay for your health care. And, again, the majority of the people, the nearly eight million Medicaid recipients are people that live in rural underserved communities, that are low-income, that are black, brown, indigenous and LGBTQ, disabled.

And, moreover, again, this is about preventative essential health care, like cancer screenings, preventative care like that which I received. When I was a young student in a city that was new to me, I had no community, no family. I was experiencing debilitating pain.

I went to Planned Parenthood and learned that I was living with multiple uterine fibroids. So it is essential care like that. And so the court needs to uphold the law and they need to rule on the side of the people. And if they don't, Alicia, it will be devastating.

It will mean a denial of diagnoses, of preventative care. People will get sicker and people will die, especially in rural and underserved communities, because they will be living in care deserts. Planned Parenthood are qualified providers.

And this is a matter of fundamental freedoms. Medicaid recipients, you know, it's their doctor, their choice, just like anybody else with private coverage.

MENENDEZ: It strikes me that the court is hearing this case and you and I are having this conversation a day after Wisconsinites elected Democratic-backed Justice Susan Crawford.

There were a number of issues in that race. It has often been sort of called a referendum on Elon Musk and on DOGE. But there were questions about voting rights. There were questions about collective bargaining. And, as you know, there were questions about abortion and reproductive care.

It seems to me that abortion as a motivating political issue is suddenly in the peripheral view in terms of our discourse, but it is my sense that it is still very much top of mind for Americans and for voters. When you were out there, when you were talking to folks, what do you hear?

PRESSLEY: Absolutely.

I mean, again, in my role as co-chair of the Reproductive Freedom Caucus, I'm daily not only engaging with activists and providers like Planned Parenthood, but hearing from everyday people who understand that this is a coordinated assault on health care.

They seek to dismantle Medicaid. They seek to advance a country with a national ban on abortion, which essentially is a nation of forced birth. And we already have too many harrowing and tragic stories of people who've narrowly lost their life or lost their life because they could not access abortion care, which is health care, when they were experiencing sepsis or bleeding out or for miscarriage management.

So, increasingly, what I find is that people are clear about the stakes. That's why so many people were motivated today. A lot of people were talking about how inspired they were by Senator Cory Booker and the stamina that he's demonstrating in this moment.

And this is an unprecedented moment for our country. And we have to match that energy with unprecedented organizing, with unprecedented agitating, with unprecedented mobilizing, with unprecedented truth-telling.

And so that's why I wanted to start with people understanding that the law is on our side. Congress passed this law nearly half-a-century ago, giving Medicaid patients the right to access health care, including routine primary care, from any qualified provider.

Alicia, we have to make the truth more compelling than the lies. There's so much mis- and dis-information out there, and that's on purpose. They want an electorate that is ignorant. They want an electorate that is indifferent and that is inactive.

But people are very awake. And they are showing up in community and organizing peacefully, just as they did today at the steps of the Supreme Court. And it is going to take the actions of everyday citizens, along with my colleagues and I, in active resistance to these assaults, because it is a matter of life and death.

MENENDEZ: Representative Pressley, I just want to admit to you and to our viewers that you began where I planned to end, which was talking about the absolute irony of this president labeling today liberation day as he is actively taking an entire series of rights away from Americans.

Representative Ayanna Pressley, thank you so much for being with us.

PRESSLEY: Thank you.

END

data.title: South Africa business activity contracts for fourth month in a row - PMI
data.description:
South Africa's private sector shrank in March for the fourth consecutive month, as output and sales declined amid economic uncertainty, a survey showed on Thursday.

The S&P Global South Africa Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) fell to 48.3 in March from 49.0 in February, remaining below the 50.0 threshold that separates growth from contraction.

This was the second-lowest reading since July 2023, indicating ongoing challenges in the business environment.

Output volumes decreased steadily, with firms attributing the decline to lower new orders, contract completions, and weaker consumer spending.

New order volumes fell sharply, driven by economic and political uncertainties, although there was a slight uptick in international demand, particularly from African countries.

"The March PMI figures confirmed a full quarter of declining business conditions in the South African private sector," said David Owen, senior economist at S&P Global Market Intelligence.

"Demand levels continued to fall in March, with some panellists blaming the uncertain outlook on domestic fiscal policy and global trade, which has contributed to a waning of business confidence and a drop in customer spending," Owen said.

On a positive note, cost pressures eased, with input price inflation reaching a five-month low. The rand's improved exchange rate helped mitigate supplier cost increases, leading to a marginal decline in selling prices for the first time since October 2024.

data.title: WTI Falls 3.4% to $69.29 a Barrel After Trump's Tariffs
Hillgrove Resources Raises AU$5 Million via Share Purchase Plan
data.description:
Hillgrove Resources raised AU$5 million via the issue of 143.8 million new shares on April 4, bringing the total capital raised, to AU$18 million, according to a Thursday Australia bourse filing.

The SPP, which targeted AU$3 million but received AU$8.2 million in subscriptions, led to a scale-back of all applications under the terms of the capital raise, the filing said.

The funds will be used to accelerate exploration and fast-track the development of the Nugent project, the filing added.

Shares of the company rose 6% on market close.

data.title: Brent Falls 3.2% to $72.58 a Barrel After Trump's Tariffs
Barclays Keeps GSK at Equal Weight, Sees Q1 Turnover in Line with Consensus
data.description:
Barclays maintained its equal weight rating on GSK , noting that the drugmaker's turnover and core EPS remain in line with expectations for the first quarter of 2025.

The research firm kept its price target of 14.50 pounds sterling, estimating GSK's group turnover at 7.55 billion pounds and core EPS at 0.423 pound for the first quarter. However, analysts lowered their forecasts for shingles and lower respiratory tract disease vaccines Shingrix and Arexvy, respectively, while expecting the specialty medicines and general medicines portfolios to offset the weakness.

"Though sentiment towards shares was quite negative at the start of the year, we're sensing a bit of a shift as enthusiasm builds (albeit slowly) for the relaunch of Blenrep later this year and it remains the cheapest stock in our large-cap coverage. We expect the call to be heavy on policy, given recent changes at HHS and headlines regarding vaccines as well as HIV prevention programs," Barclays wrote.

Looking ahead, analysts expect the company's vaccines portfolio to face challenges tied to comparisons and script trends. Meanwhile, cost of goods sold is expected to improve, excluding tariffs, with GSK having minimal exposure in Canada, Mexico, and China.

data.title: Malaysian REITs Likely to See Strong Earnings Growth in 1QMarket Talk
data.description: The Malaysian real-estate investment trust sector may maintain a steady performance in 1Q, supported by stable interest rates and strong earnings growth, CIMB Securities analysts Lim Yue Jia and Ivy Ng Lee Fang say in a note. Sector earnings are projected to rise 11% on-quarter in 1Q, driven by new asset contributions, the reopening of refurbished areas, and festive spending, they reckon. Despite potential headwinds from subsidy reforms in 2H, global uncertainties and recent market volatility, the sector is poised to benefit from rising tourism, boosted by government incentives ahead of Visit Malaysia 2026, they add. CIMB maintains a neutral rating on Malaysian REITs and pegs Axis Real Estate Investment Trust and Sunway REIT as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Turkish Producer Prices Rise the Least Since 2020
data.description:
The annual producer inflation in Turkey dropped to 23.50% in March 2025, down from 25.21% in the previous month, marking the tenth consecutive month of deceleration.

This was also the lowest reading since November 2020, as most prices in the sector moderated: manufacturing (23.14% vs. 24.76% in February), mining and quarrying (30.89% vs. 33.34%), and electricity, gas, and steam (21.15% vs. 23.95%).

Meanwhile, prices quickened for water supply (50.02% vs. 57.58%).

On a monthly basis, producer prices rose by 1.88%, the least in three months, easing from a 2.12% gain in the previous month.

data.title: UK 10Y Bond Yield Hits 4-week Low
data.description:
UK 10 Year Government Bond Yield decreased to a 4-week low of 4.55%.

Over the past 4 weeks, United Kingdom 10Y Bond Yield gained 16.02 basis points, and in the last 12 months, it increased 63.31 basis points.

data.title: Sporting goods makers Adidas, Puma slump after Trump announces tariffs
data.description:
Shares in Adidas and Puma dropped sharply on Thursday after U.S. President Donald Trump imposed a raft of new tariffs, including on key sourcing markets for the sporting goods companies.

The European Union was targeted with a 20% rate, Vietnam with 46%, Cambodia with 49%, Bangladesh with 37% and Indonesia with 32%, while China is facing 54% tariffs.

Adidas was down 9% to nearly one-year low, Puma slumped 11% to its lowest since November 2016 at 0706 GMT.

Frankfurt-listed shares of Nike were also down 6.5% on the day.

data.title: Oil Slumps on Demand Fears After Trump's Tariff Blitz
data.description:
By Giulia Petroni

Oil prices fell sharply after U.S. President Trump unveiled plans to impose sweeping tariffs on major trade partners, stoking fears that an escalating global trade war will hurt economic growth and demand for crude.

In early European trade, Brent dropped 3.2% to $72.53 a barrel, while West Texas Intermediate was down 3.4% at $69.24 a barrel.

The U.S. will impose a 10% minimum tariff on most imported goods and much higher duties on some nations--including a 34% reciprocal tariff on China and 20% tariff on the European Union.

The move has raised fears of rising inflation and a potential slowdown in global economic activity, with trading partners expected to retaliate with their own measures.

Canada and Mexico, the U.S.'s largest suppliers of oil, were excluded from the reciprocal tariffs. Imports of oil, gas and refined products were also reportedly exempted.

Write to Giulia Petroni at giulia.petroni@wsj.com

data.title: Primary Health Proposes $1.95 Billion Bid for Assura
data.description:
By Anthony O. Goriainoff

Primary Health said it made an indicative share and cash takeover proposal for Assura which values the healthcare property investor and developer at around 1.5 billion pounds ($1.95 billion), below a previous bid made by a KKR and Stonepeak Partners consortium.

The U.K. real-estate investment company said its offer would entail Assura shareholders receiving 0.3848 new PHP shares as well as 9.08 pence in cash for each share in Assura. This combination implied an initial value of 46.2 pence a share, a 24% premium to Assura's closing share price on Feb. 13, the last business day before the company entering an offer period. The offer represents an 0.6% discount to Wednesday's closing price of 46.48 pence.

Primary Health said Assura shareholders would also retain the 0.84 pence quarterly dividend.

The combination of both companies would deliver significant strategic and financial benefits for their respective shareholders, Primary Health said.

On March 10 Assura said it received an improved 1.61 billion pounds takeover approach from the KKR and Stonepeak Partners consortium which it would be prepared to recommend to shareholders if a formal proposal was made. The new offer was for 49.4 pence a share, a premium of nearly 32% to the stock's closing price on Feb. 13.

Write to Anthony O. Goriainoff at anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com

data.title: ChiNext Price Index Ends 1.9% Lower at 2065.40
South Korea may open up local crypto market to foreign investors if AML strengthens: report
data.description:
South Korea's top financial regulator said it could possibly open the doors for foreigners to invest in the local cryptocurrency market under the condition that domestic exchanges equip sufficient anti-money laundering capabilities.

Kim Sung-jin, the head of the virtual asset division under the Financial Services Commission, spoke during a Wednesday seminar at the National Assembly that he "agrees with" the direction that foreign investors should be allowed to access the domestic crypto market, local news outlet Bloter reported. Kim reportedly added that the commission will consider ways to attract global investors into the local market — and that current conditions could change if exchanges qualify regarding AML capabilities.

Foreign investors are currently restricted from trading on South Korean crypto exchanges, as local rules require strict know-your-customer standards on service providers. Local exchange users who want to trade cash-to-crypto need to use local bank accounts registered under their real names. "Korea imposes capital account restrictions — control over portfolio investments," said Peter Chung, Head of Research at Presto Research. "Allowing foreigners to trade crypto on Korean exchanges would mute such restrictions."

South Korea appears to be re-evaluating its long-standing capital controls to keep pace with the global trend of crypto innovation being led by the U.S. administration under President Trump. Chung told The Block that South Korea's potential unlocking of the local crypto market could bring more vitality to its crypto industry, as well as spur further growth of the USD stablecoin market. This would also eliminate the Kimchi Premium, which is the discrepancy between the price of cryptocurrencies on local exchanges and elsewhere, as global liquidity is restricted from entering the market.

Chung added that South Korean regulators currently view that local exchanges are not yet equipped to handle AML duties sufficiently, which is implied in the FSC official's latest comments. Prior to enforcing KYC requirements and establishing the country's first-ever crypto legal framework, the FSC put an AML measure in place in March 2022 by adopting the Travel Rule. The rule, aligned with the Financial Action Task Force's recommendation, mandates that exchanges collect and store information about the sender and recipient of crypto transactions above the amount of one million won ($681.38). Major Korean exchanges recently notified that they will apply the Travel Rule on transactions below the threshold. 

Earlier this year, the Financial Intelligence Unit of South Korea imposed a penalty on Upbit, the largest cryptocurrency exchange in the country, for allegedly supporting transactions with dozens of unregistered foreign exchanges. The penalty, however, has been halted after a Seoul court granted Upbit's injunction.

South Korea is home to one of the largest cryptocurrency markets in the world — which is also known as being altcoin-heavy. In March, Upbit had over $85 billion in monthly trade volume.

Disclaimer: The Block is an independent media outlet that delivers news, research, and data. As of November 2023, Foresight Ventures is a majority investor of The Block. Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. The Block continues to operate independently to deliver objective, impactful, and timely information about the crypto industry. Here are our current financial disclosures.

© 2025 The Block. All Rights Reserved. This article is provided for informational purposes only. It is not offered or intended to be used as legal, tax, investment, financial, or other advice.

data.title: Swiss Inflation Holds Steady as Tariffs Set to Buffet Economy
data.description:
By Ed Frankl

Swiss inflation was unchanged in March, though the impact of U.S. tariffs on imports threaten to roil the country's economy and push the Swiss National Bank to cut rates further.

Annual inflation stayed at 0.3%, the same as February, matching the lowest level in almost four years, Switzerland's federal statistics office said Thursday.

Trends from increased package-holiday and clothing prices contrasted with declining energy and accommodation prices, it said. Core inflation, which excludes more volatile fresh food and energy prices, remained markedly higher, staying at 0.9% on year for the third-straight month.

But new U.S tariffs announced Wednesday by President Trump are expected to significantly hit the Swiss economy, though its impact on inflation is less predictable.

The Trump administration imposed a 31% tariff on Swiss exports to the U.S., higher than the 20% for the European Union and 10% for the U.K. The U.S. is Switzerland's largest goods-export market.

The SNB cut its key interest rate in March to 0.25%, citing increased geopolitical uncertainty, as well as risks that inflation would go lower. SNB President Martin Schlegel in recent months hasn't ruled out negative interest rates.

Swiss President Karin Keller-Sutter said her government took note of the new duties and would decide soon how to proceed.

"The country's long-term economic interests are paramount. Respect to international law and free trade remain core values," she said on X.

Swiss industry group Economiesuisse said Thursday the new tariffs were harmful and unjustified, will make Swiss exports more expensive and would weaken competitiveness.

Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com
